title: Chemical accidents rise as Trump administration proposes weakening safety rules
gist: A PEER analysis shows US dangerous-chemical accidents rose 57% (83 to 131) from 2021-2025 while the Trump EPA proposes to weaken the 2024 Risk Management Program safety rules, defund the Chemical Safety Board, and has removed public risk-disclosure tools. Refineries using hydrofluoric acid near dense population centers are the flagged catastrophic-tail risk.

insights.claim: US accidents involving releases of dangerous chemicals rose 57 percent between 2021 and 2025, climbing from 83 to 131 per year, per a Public Employees for Environmental Responsibility analysis.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: A rising incident trend line contradicts the industry/EPA 'declining releases' narrative and raises tail liability for refiners and chemical operators; watch as ammunition in the RMP rulemaking fight.
insights.quote: The number of accidents involving releases of dangerous chemicals rose by 57 percent between 2021 and 2025, from 83 to 131
insights.locator: The number of accidents involving releases

insights.claim: Injuries or deaths from chemical accidents rose from 60 to 89 over the 2021-2025 period, tracking the rise in incident frequency.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: Escalating human-harm figures strengthen the case for tighter regulation and higher expected litigation/settlement costs for facility operators.
insights.quote: Injuries or deaths from accidents also rose, from 60 to 89 over the same five-year period

insights.claim: Chemical Safety Board reports show more than 650 accidents between April 2020 and May 2026, including 103 fatal, 355 causing injuries, and 314 causing substantial property damage.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: Establishes the base rate of catastrophic industrial exposure across the regulated universe an investor should price into insurance and operational-risk models.
insights.quote: more than 650 accidents occurred between April 2020 and May 2026, with 103 resulting in fatalities, 355 causing injuries and 314 doing “substantial property damage.”
insights.locator: Incident reports released by the Chemical Safety Board

insights.claim: Chemical accidents causing evacuations, injuries, or multiple casualties are occurring at least once a week in the US.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: A weekly cadence of serious events signals a structurally elevated, ongoing operational-risk backdrop rather than isolated tail events.
insights.quote: chemical accidents resulting in evacuations, injuries, or multiple casualties continue to happen at least once a week
insights.locator: Meanwhile, chemical accidents resulting in evacuations

insights.claim: Close to 150 million people live within 3 miles of facilities handling these hazardous chemicals, with Black and Latino populations at greatest exposure risk.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: The scale of exposed population underpins environmental-justice litigation and community opposition risk that can stall permits and expansions near population centers.
insights.quote: Close to 150 million people live within 3 miles of these facilities.

insights.claim: In 1980s Amoco-funded tests, releasing 1,000 gallons of hydrofluoric acid produced a ground-hugging mist that carried the deadly gas miles downwind, far beyond the expected small vapor pool.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: The dispersion physics mean HF units near cities carry mass-casualty tail risk that plant-boundary assumptions understate; a driver for phase-out pressure and retrofit capex.
insights.quote: a billowing “ground-hugging” mist formed, allowing the deadly gas to travel miles downwind, considerably farther than anyone thought possible.

insights.claim: The 2019 Philadelphia Energy Solutions refinery explosion released more than 5,000 pounds of hydrofluoric acid and a nearby dense neighborhood was spared only by favorable wind conditions.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: A near-miss mass-casualty event that reliance on 'favorable wind' underscores the un-hedged catastrophic risk embedded in urban HF refineries.
insights.quote: The neighboring mostly Black and brown South Philadelphia neighborhood was spared thanks to “favorable wind conditions,” the CSB said.
insights.locator: The accident released more than 5,000 pounds

insights.claim: Exposure to 170 parts per million of hydrogen fluoride for 10 minutes can cause death or serious injury.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: Quantifies the lethality threshold that makes HF releases a low-probability, extreme-severity liability distinct from ordinary industrial hazards.
insights.quote: Exposure to 170 parts per million of hydrogen fluoride for 10 minutes can cause death or serious injury.

insights.claim: Close to 50 US refineries use hydrogen fluoride and have reported over 200 serious-injury or fatal accidents to the EPA over 25 years.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: Identifies a concentrated ~50-asset cohort carrying disproportionate regulatory and liability exposure if HF phase-out momentum builds.
insights.quote: Close to 50 refineries use hydrogen fluoride and have reported more than 200 accidents resulting in serious injuries and deaths to the EPA over the past 25 years
insights.locator: Close to 50 refineries use hydrogen fluoride

insights.claim: Aging infrastructure is a compounding driver of chemical-accident risk, with many refineries built before 1985 and risk rising each year as plants age.
insights.speaker: Jeff Ruch
insights.so_what: Structural aging implies rising baseline accident probability and mounting maintenance/retrofit capex needs across the refining base.
insights.quote: With each passing year the risk gets greater because the infrastructure continues to age

insights.claim: The Trump EPA proposed to significantly weaken the 2024 Risk Management Program rules 'to reduce regulatory burden,' with a final rule targeted for late 2026.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: A weaker RMP lowers near-term compliance capex for chemical/refining operators but raises long-tail liability and reversal risk under future administrations; the late-2026 final rule is the key catalyst to watch.
insights.quote: the administration proposed to significantly weaken RMP rules finalized in 2024 “to reduce regulatory burden”
insights.locator: Earlier this year, the administration proposed

insights.claim: The 2024 Biden RMP rules that Trump seeks to weaken mandated safer-alternatives analyses, independent root-cause accident analyses, worker participation in prevention plans, and climate-adaptation preparations.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: These are the specific compliance obligations at stake; their removal directly reduces operator cost but marks the exact provisions a future re-tightening would restore.
insights.quote: safer-alternatives analyses, independent analyses of accidents’ root causes, worker participation in accident-prevention plans and preparations to adapt to climate change.
insights.locator: The Biden administration’s strengthened RMP rules

insights.claim: The EPA's Risk Management Program under the Clean Air Act regulates roughly 12,000 facilities that use certain hazardous substances, of which HF refineries are only a fraction.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: Sizes the total regulated universe affected by any RMP change, defining the breadth of the deregulation trade beyond refining.
insights.quote: the 12,000 facilities that use certain hazardous substances and are regulated by the EPA’s Risk Management Program under the Clean Air Act.
insights.locator: The refineries represent just a fraction

insights.claim: The Trump administration has moved to eliminate the Chemical Safety Board by withholding funding and removed a public data tool designed to inform communities of nearby chemical risks, though Congress has kept funding the CSB.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: Shrinking federal oversight and disclosure reduces public visibility into incidents, shifting risk-monitoring burden to investors and raising the value of independent data sources like PEER.
insights.quote: Trump’s EPA removed a public data tool designed to inform communities of nearby risks last year.
insights.locator: Yet Trump’s EPA removed a public data tool

themes.theme: Data Interpretation Dispute and Transparency Fights

insights.claim: The EPA claims RMP-reportable incidents from 2014-2023 show accidental releases 'unequivocally declined significantly,' arguing prevention programs worked before the 2024 rule.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: The government's official position is the rollback's legal basis; investors should track whether courts accept the 'declining releases' framing against PEER's contradicting trend.
insights.quote: accidental releases unequivocally declined significantly over that period
insights.locator: EPA’s proposal relies on a rigorous analysis

insights.claim: PEER argues the Biden EPA drew the opposite conclusion from the same dataset, and that attributing any decline to industry prevention plans is unsupported supposition.
insights.speaker: Jeff Ruch
insights.so_what: The same-data/opposite-conclusion clash signals the rulemaking is vulnerable to legal challenge on evidentiary grounds, a reversal risk for any deregulation trade.
insights.quote: the conclusion that any decline is due to industry prevention plans is a supposition which the current EPA does not have the data to support.
insights.locator: The Biden EPA used the same data

insights.claim: PEER's accident statistics were only made public through a lawsuit compelling the Chemical Safety Board to disclose industrial releases, after a 2019 federal ruling that communities have a right to know nearby hazardous releases.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: Litigation-forced disclosure shows incident data availability is contested and legally hard-won, meaning underlying risk may be systematically underreported in public filings.
insights.quote: a lawsuit PEER and other groups filed to compel the Chemical Safety Board to disclose industrial chemical releases as required by the Clean Air Act.
insights.locator: The new statistics released by PEER

insights.claim: PEER petitioned the EPA to ban hydrogen fluoride in 2019 after the Philadelphia explosion, but the agency refused to consider the petition.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: Prior failed phase-out attempts show HF-ban risk is recurring but not imminent; a policy option that could resurface under a future administration.
insights.quote: PEER petitioned the EPA to ban hydrogen fluoride in 2019. The agency refused to consider the petition.
insights.locator: After the massive Philadelphia refinery explosion
